Novak Djokovic’s 2026 Wimbledon campaign came to an end with a straight-sets defeat against Jannik Sinner in the semifinals.
Djokovic’s loss naturally raised questions about his future in professional tennis, particularly given his age and the significance of Wimbledon in his legendary career.
Novak Djokovic has not announced his retirement following his semifinal loss to Jannik Sinner at Wimbledon 2026.
Despite the disappointment, Djokovic’s comments indicate that he still has the motivation to compete at the highest level.
His desire to return to Wimbledon in 2027 suggests that he still believes he can compete with the best players in the world, even after a difficult loss to Jannik Sinner."},{"url":"https://www.express.co.uk/sport/tennis/2228508/Novak-Djokovic-retire-Wimbledon","title":"Should Novak Djokovic retire after Wimbledon? Vote now on Serb's future | Tennis | Sport | Express.co.uk","content":"5 days ago — Djokovic replied: “I would like to, at least one more time. Let's see.” “Was. Was. That's the reality. Was. Was.”
When asked following his 6-4 6-4 6-4 semi-final defeat to Sinner if he intends to return to Wimbledon next year as a 40-year-old, Djokovic replied: “I would like to, at least one more time.
Such comments have left many questioning whether Djokovic should retire, as he appears to be drifting further away from Sinner and, when healthy again, Carlos Alcaraz.
Novak Djokovic waved to the crowd after losing to Jannik Sinner.

While Djokovic hit a level of consistency in the 2025 season by reaching the semi-finals of all four Grand Slams, the Serbian has not been able to maintain that level of form this year.
Since, Djokovic has suffered early exits at Indian Wells, the Italian Open, and Roland Garros, the latter of which saw him fail to capitalise on the early exit of Jannik Sinner and Carlos Alcaraz’s withdrawal.
“I was asked before Roland Garros whether I saw Djokovic winning the tournament, and I answered no; not because I don’t consider him good enough, but because the years go by,” said Nadal.
Nadal believes the only reason Djokovic is yet to retire from tennis is due to his quest to try and win a 25th Grand Slam, but he believes his time in tennis is going to come to an end very soon.
Nadal might not be tipping Djokovic to do well at Wimbledon, but history dictates the Serbian star will at least make the latter stages of the Grand Slam.
